mnemonics.title: Sequential Access Characteristics
mnemonics.content: S-E-Q: S-tarts from E-very X-ord (beginning), processes in order, X-cludes random access. Remember: SEQUENTIAL = SEQUENCE MATTERS

mnemonics.title: Index Types
mnemonics.content: DENSE = EVERY record gets a POINTER. SPARSE = FEWER pointers, points to BLOCKS. DENSE has More Index Entries.

mnemonics.title: Access Method Selection
mnemonics.content: TAPE = T-apes A-ccess P-hysically E-verything S-equentially. Choose Sequential for tapes, Direct/Indexed for disks.

mnemonics.title: Hash Collision Handling
mnemonics.content: L-O-O-P: Linear Probing, Open Addressing, Overflow chaining, P-robing variants (quadratic). Alternative: C-haining A-llows R-ehashing (CAR).

acronyms.usage: Used for magnetic tapes and batch processing where records are processed in order
Used for disks allowing O(1) access via relative record numbers
Combines sequential storage with multi-level indexing for fast random and sequential access
Logical identifier for records in direct access; RRN 1 is first record, RRN n is nth record
Data structure used for indexes with all pointers at leaf level, enabling efficient search and range queries

visualTips.visualization: Think of a VHS TAPE or audio cassette - to watch a scene in the middle, you must fast-forward through all previous scenes. Records flow like water in a pipe - one after another in sequence.

Think of a DVD or Blu-ray where you can jump directly to any chapter. Like an array where each element has a known index address. Picture a bookshelf with numbered positions - go directly to shelf 5, position 3.
Think of a book INDEX at the back - to find a topic, you look up the word first (index), get the page number, then turn to that page. The index is a shortcut to the actual data.

Think of an organizational chart - starts wide at top, narrows down as you go down. Each level splits the data into smaller groups. To find any employee, you navigate from CEO → Department → Team → Person. Leaf nodes contain actual data pointers.
Two different car license plates (keys) somehow map to the same parking slot (bucket). Need a rule: maybe go to next slot (linear probing), or have a line of cars in one slot (chaining).

analogies.analogy: Reading a book from page 1 to page 300 in order, like watching a movie on VHS where you must fast-forward to reach a specific scene.
Using an elevator in a building - you press button for floor 5 and go directly there, without visiting floors 1-4 first.
Using a dictionary - you don't read all words sequentially; you use the guide words at the top of pages to directly jump to the word you want.
Hotel room assignment - guest name (key) is processed through a formula (hash function) to determine room number (address). Multiple guests might need same room, requiring conflict resolution.
A table of contents in a textbook - it doesn't contain the full information but tells you exactly which page to find each topic on.
Dense index is like a book's index with EVERY word listed with page numbers. Sparse index is like floor directory in a mall - lists stores on each floor, not every single shop.
